text:
By 2023, CP All operated roughly 14,000 7-Eleven locations across Thailand. Its SET-listed annual filings reported over 14 million transactions per day across the network. That is one store for approximately every 5,000 people in the country. These locations are not just retail. They are embedded into how workers receive paychecks, pay utility bills, and access basic financial services. In Thailand, 7-Eleven is infrastructure.

CP Group was not finished.

In December 2020, as the pandemic reshaped global retail balance sheets, CP Group paid approximately 10.6 billion US dollars to acquire Tesco's hypermarket operations in Thailand and Malaysia. Tesco's press release at the time confirmed agreement to sell its businesses in Thailand and Malaysia for total consideration of approximately 8.2 billion pounds. CP Group, flush with agri-food cash flows, paid while competitors could not. The deal brought roughly 2,000 hypermarket and superstore locations under CP control.

The acquisition, rebranded as Lotus's, positioned CP Group at the intersection of fresh produce, packaged goods, and everyday household retail. Combined with its food-processing operations upstream, the company now controlled product origination, wholesale supply, and end-consumer sale within a single structure.

Then, in 2023, True Corporation, a CP Group entity, completed its merger with DTAC, the Danish-owned mobile operator. The combined network serves approximately 52 million subscribers in Thailand. For the first time, a single Thai conglomerate controlled convenience retail at national scale, the national food-supply chain at the production end, and a major mobile network.

Read that sequence again. One ownership structure has visibility into what a Thai consumer eats, where they buy staples, and which network connects their device.

The question analysts rarely ask is why this architecture receives so little attention outside the region. CP All and True are publicly traded on the Stock Exchange of Thailand with fully audited filings. The conglomerate structure is not unusual for Asia. What is unusual is the scale of the vertical coverage and how rarely that coverage is mapped against what Amazon, Alibaba, and their Western equivalents have spent a decade trying to assemble in developed markets.

The Forbes 2024 wealth rankings estimated Dhanin Chearavanont's family net worth at approximately 14.4 billion US dollars. That number is a financial metric. It does not capture the embedded commercial position that the businesses represent.

The seed shop in Yaowarat is now a historical reference in CP Group's corporate materials. The structure it founded controls a larger share of daily Thai commercial life than any other single entity, domestic or foreign. That is not an accident either. It is a century of deliberate decisions about which step in the value chain to own next.

edited_script.title: CP Group Part 2: Retail, Telecom, and the Hidden Stack
edited_script.description: CP Group completes its vertical stack via Tesco Lotus acquisition, 14,000 7-Eleven stores, and the True+DTAC merger.

visuals.prompt: Infographic data visualization: Thailand country map densely covered with small green store-pin icons representing 14,000 locations. High density visible around Bangkok metro area, coverage spreading to all provinces. Bold statistics callout panel beside the map: "14,000 stores" and "14M transactions/day." Clean white background, editorial corporate style. No human figures. NEGATIVE: no agricultural imagery, no seeds, no poultry, no processing facilities. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.

Cinematic wide establishing shot of Bangkok skyline at dusk. Corporate office towers reflecting orange and purple sunset light across the Chao Phraya river. Atmospheric haze on the horizon. text_overlays: What you eat, buy, and connect on

Glowing connection lines converge from all three nodes toward a single central hub point. Deep dark navy background with bright neon-teal connection lines. Corporate infographic editorial style. No human figures, no brand logos, no product images. NEGATIVE: no photorealistic product imagery, no company logos, no food items, no store exteriors. Single hero subject. One unified cinematic frame. No collage, no multi-panel layout, no grid composition.
